title: Course Syllabus
text: ECO 1000 · Principles of Economics · Fall 2026

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Course Overview
This course introduces students to the fundamental principles of economics. We examine how individuals, firms, and governments make decisions in a world of scarcity, and how those decisions shape markets, prices, and living standards.
Credits: 3 Meeting times: [Add your meeting days/times here] Location: [Add your classroom here]

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Instructor Information



Instructor
Prof. Brandon Sheridan


Office
KoBC 118


Email
bsheridan4@elon.edu


Office Hours
[Add your office hours here]

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Course Objectives
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

Apply the core principles of scarcity, trade-offs, and opportunity cost to real-world decisions.
Analyze supply and demand and explain how markets reach equilibrium.
Evaluate the effects of government policies such as taxes, subsidies, and price controls.
Distinguish between microeconomic and macroeconomic perspectives.
Interpret basic economic data and connect course concepts to current events.

title: Course Syllabus

Course Policies

Attendance
Regular attendance is expected. Economics builds on itself — missing class makes it harder to keep up. Please notify me in advance if you must miss a session.


Late Work
[Add your late work policy here.]


Academic Integrity
All work you submit must be your own. Violations of Elon’s Honor Code will be reported. See also the AI in this Course page for guidance on using AI tools.


Accessibility
If you have a documented disability and need accommodations, please contact Disabilities Resources and reach out to me early in the semester.

title: AI in this Course
text: ECO 1000 · Principles of Economics · Fall 2026

title: AI in this Course
text:
My Approach to AI Tools
AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and others are increasingly part of how people work and learn. In this course, I want to help you develop a thoughtful, intentional relationship with these tools — one that supports your learning rather than bypassing it.

title: AI in this Course
text:
Policy






Important



The default in this course is that all submitted work should be your own. Any use of AI tools beyond what is explicitly permitted on a given assignment is a violation of Elon’s Honor Code.


For each assignment, I will specify what level of AI use (if any) is appropriate. There are three categories:







Category
What it means




No AI
The work must be entirely your own. AI use of any kind is not permitted.


AI as a reference
You may use AI to clarify concepts or check your understanding, but the writing and analysis must be yours. Any AI use must be disclosed.


AI-assisted
You may use AI more actively in your process. Full disclosure of how you used it is required.



If an assignment does not specify, assume No AI.

title: AI in this Course
text:
Why These Guidelines?
Economics is a discipline that rewards careful thinking and the ability to apply abstract concepts to specific situations. AI tools can generate plausible-sounding answers very quickly — but that’s not the same as understanding. The assignments in this course are designed to help you build that understanding. Outsourcing the thinking defeats the purpose.
That said, AI tools are real, they’re not going away, and knowing how to use them well is genuinely valuable. I’d rather help you develop good habits now than pretend these tools don’t exist.

title: AI in this Course
text:
Disclosure
Whenever AI use is permitted, you must include a brief disclosure at the end of your submission. Example:

I used [tool name] to [describe how you used it]. The analysis and writing are my own.

Failure to disclose AI use when it occurred is treated as academic dishonesty, regardless of whether the assignment permitted AI use.

title: AI in this Course
text:
Questions?
If you’re ever unsure whether a particular use of AI is appropriate for a given assignment, ask me before submitting — not after. I’m happy to talk through specific situations. Reach me at bsheridan4@elon.edu.

title: Help & Support
text:
Tutoring & Academic Support
Elon’s Koenigsberger Learning Center (KLC) offers free tutoring for many courses:

Website: elon.edu/klc
Location: Belk Library, Lower Level
Peer tutors are available — check the KLC website for economics tutoring availability.

title: Help & Support
text:
Study Tips for Economics

Keep up with the readings. Economics concepts build on each other — falling behind makes catching up harder.
Work through practice problems. Reading about supply and demand is different from solving a problem yourself.
Connect concepts to the real world. When you hear something in the news, ask yourself how it relates to what we’ve covered in class.
Form a study group. Explaining concepts to others is one of the best ways to test your own understanding.
Come to office hours early. Don’t wait until the night before an exam.
